summary: Tractate Yoma daf 84a covers: Killing a mad dog; signs of madness (symptoms that define a dangerous dog); R. Matya ben Harash: throat pain on YK permits taking medicine; Tzefardea (snake-related illness): what it is, its symptoms, its danger.

display.whats: Killing a mad dog; signs of madness (symptoms that define a dangerous dog)
display.hint: The Gemara discusses how to deal with a mad dog and what signs indicate rabies. The mad dog may be killed even on Shabbat if it poses immediate danger.
display.title: Identifying a Rabid Dog: Signs of Madness

learning.ahaMoment: The Gemara is being practical: you cannot invoke pikuach nefesh to kill the dog unless you can identify it as dangerous. The symptom list makes recognition a legal competence.
learning.memoryAnchor: Signs of a mad dog (open mouth, dripping saliva, flapping ears): recognize them and the animal may be killed on Shabbat.
learning.learnerQuestion: How do you identify a mad (rabid) dog, and may it be killed even on Shabbat?
learning.coreTension: Between the prohibition on killing animals on Shabbat and the danger that a rabid dog poses to anyone it encounters.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara details the signs of rabies (mouth open, saliva dripping, tail hanging, ears flopping, gait off) and rules that a dog showing these signs may be killed even on Shabbat, because leaving it alive constitutes ongoing danger to human life. Recognizing danger is the prerequisite for acting on it.

takeaway.text: A potentially rabid animal may be killed on Shabbat (and YK) because leaving it alive constitutes ongoing threat to human life; the specific signs must be identified to apply this override, making recognition a halakhic skill.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may not realize that the Gemara's list of symptoms is a practical diagnostic tool embedded in halakhic law.

argumentFlow.label: Kill a mad dog only from a distance
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Following Shmuel's view that an evil spirit rests on the mad dog, one kills it only with a thrown object, like an arrow, avoiding direct contact.

argumentFlow.label: Rubbing versus biting
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: A baraita aligned with Shmuel: one merely rubbed by a mad dog becomes dangerously ill, while one bitten will die. The remedy for one who is rubbed is to strip off his clothing and run, as Rav Huna son of Rav Yehoshua did in the market.

argumentFlow.label: The elaborate cure for a bite
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: For one actually bitten, Abaye prescribes an elaborate remedy: writing a spell on hyena skin with divine names, then burying his clothes in a cemetery for twelve months, burning them, and scattering the ashes.

theological: The signs of a mad dog are listed as specific observable symptoms - the Talmud t
narrative: Killing a mad dog; signs of madness (symptoms that define a

requiresUnderstanding: Background knowledge of mad dog context
Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: The remedies for a mad dog bite reflect ordinary medical treatment.
misconceptions.correction: These are folk-magical remedies tied to the belief that an evil spirit possesses the dog, not standard medicine - the halachic point is the danger and the avoidance of contact.

visualizableElements.name: Killing a mad dog; signs of madness (symptoms that

quizSeeds.question: Why is a mad dog killed only with a thrown object rather than by hand?
quizSeeds.answer: Following Shmuel, an evil spirit rests on the dog, so one avoids direct contact and kills it from a distance, such as with an arrow.

quizSeeds.question: What is the difference between being rubbed by a mad dog and being bitten?
quizSeeds.answer: One merely rubbed becomes dangerously ill and is treated by stripping and running, while one actually bitten will die and requires the elaborate remedy Abaye describes.

sugyot.finalRuling: The Gemara discusses how to deal with a mad dog and what signs indicate rabies. The mad dog may be killed even on Shabbat if it poses immediate danger

display.whats: R. Matya ben Harash: throat pain on YK permits taking medicine
display.hint: R. Matya ben Harash rules that one suffering from throat pain on Yom Kippur may take medicine, as the throat is near vital organs and danger to life is present.
display.title: R. Matya ben Harash: Throat Pain Permits Medicine

learning.ahaMoment: The throat's proximity to vital organs is not a medical fact added to halakha - it is the halakhic fact. Anatomy determines the legal threshold here.
learning.memoryAnchor: Throat pain on YK: treat with medicine immediately - anatomical proximity to vital organs raises the stakes.
learning.learnerQuestion: Why does R. Matya ben Harash rule that throat pain on Yom Kippur permits taking medicine?
learning.coreTension: Between the general rule that minor pain does not override YK and R. Matya ben Harash's specific ruling about throat pain.
learning.coreMove: R. Matya ben Harash rules that the throat is anatomically near vital organs; throat infection or pain can quickly progress to life-threatening conditions. This proximity justifies treating throat symptoms with medicine immediately, without waiting for the condition to deteriorate into obvious danger.

takeaway.text: The anatomical proximity of the throat to vital organs means throat pain on YK justifies immediate medical treatment; halakhic application of pikuach nefesh tracks medical reality about which conditions can rapidly become life-threatening.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may think all pain requires the same level of danger before treatment on YK; R. Matya's ruling shows that anatomy shapes the halakhic calculus.

argumentFlow.label: Medicine for throat pain on Shabbat
argumentFlow.speaker: R. Matya ben Harash
argumentFlow.text: The Mishnah's Rabbi Matya ben Charash permits placing medicine in the mouth of one with throat pain on Shabbat. The Gemara brings the case of Rabbi Yochanan, who suffered tzefidna, a gum-and-intestine illness, and was treated by a gentile healer on Thursday and Friday.

argumentFlow.label: The vow and what to do on Shabbat
argumentFlow.speaker: R. Matya ben Harash
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Yochanan asked the healer what to do on Shabbat when he could not come for the remedy. She made him swear not to reveal it, then disclosed it; the Gemara examines how he could later publicize a remedy he had sworn to keep secret.

argumentFlow.label: Resolving the vow and the chillul Hashem
argumentFlow.speaker: R. Matya ben Harash
argumentFlow.text: He had sworn not to reveal it 'to the God of the Jews,' implying he could reveal it to the Jewish people; and to avoid desecrating God's name, he had told her at the outset that his vow would not stop him from publicizing the cure.

theological: Throat pain overrides YK because the throat is adjacent to vital organs - proxim
narrative: R. Matya ben Harash: throat pain on YK permits taking medici

requiresUnderstanding: Background knowledge of R. Matya ben Harash context
Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Rabbi Yochanan broke his oath to the healer by publicizing the remedy.
misconceptions.correction: He did not break it: his vow was worded so as to permit revealing the cure to the Jewish people, and he stated this openly to the healer beforehand to avoid any desecration of God's name.

visualizableElements.name: R. Matya ben Harash: throat pain on YK permits tak

quizSeeds.question: What does the case of Rabbi Yochanan's tzefidna illustrate about Rabbi Matya ben Charash's ruling?
quizSeeds.answer: That medicine may be placed in the mouth on Shabbat for an internal illness, since tzefidna, which begins in the mouth but reaches the intestines, is potentially life-threatening.

quizSeeds.question: How does the Gemara reconcile Rabbi Yochanan publicizing the remedy with his vow of secrecy?
quizSeeds.answer: He swore only not to reveal it 'to the God of the Jews,' implying he could reveal it to the Jewish people, and he had warned the healer at the outset that the vow would not bind him.

sugyot.finalRuling: R. Matya ben Harash rules that one suffering from throat pain on Yom Kippur may take medicine, as the throat is near vital organs and danger to life i

display.whats: Tzefardea (snake-related illness): what it is, its symptoms, its danger
display.hint: The Gemara discusses 'tzefardea,' a serious illness apparently related to snake or frog venom, describing its symptoms and confirming that it is life-threatening.
display.title: Tzefardea: A Dangerous Illness

learning.ahaMoment: The Gemara is doing something remarkable: it is doing medical evaluation as halakhic practice. Knowing the disease's lethality is itself required halakhic knowledge.
learning.memoryAnchor: Tzefardea: classified dangerous because medical evidence says so. Pikuach nefesh is evidence-based.
learning.learnerQuestion: What is tzefardea and why does the Gemara treat it as a dangerous condition that overrides Yom Kippur?
learning.coreTension: Between ordinary illness (which does not automatically override YK) and the specific condition called tzefardea, which the Gemara classifies as immediately dangerous.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara describes tzefardea (apparently a condition related to snake or frog venom, or a specific pharyngeal infection) and its symptoms. It classifies it as life-threatening based on medical evidence from authorities who recognized it as rapidly fatal without treatment.

takeaway.text: The classification of an illness as YK-overriding follows empirical medical evidence; the Gemara's tzefardea ruling shows that halakhic application of pikuach nefesh is evidence-based, tracking actual medical danger rather than relying on intuition.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may not know what tzefardea is; the key point is that halakhic emergency classification requires knowing the medical facts, not just applying a general rule.

argumentFlow.label: What is tzefidna, and is it life-threatening?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Tzefidna comes from eating overly hot wheat bread and oily fish remains; its sign is that something placed between the teeth draws blood from the gums. The Gemara asks how Rabbi Yochanan treated it on Shabbat if it only affects the gums.

argumentFlow.label: Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak: it is dangerous
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak answers that tzefidna is different: though it begins in the mouth, it descends to the intestines and is therefore a genuine danger to life that overrides Shabbat.

argumentFlow.label: On whose authority did Rabbi Yochanan act?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rav Chiyya bar Abba asked Rabbi Yochanan whether he relied on Rabbi Matya ben Charash's minority view. Rabbi Yochanan answered that the Sages agreed with Rabbi Matya specifically about mouth medicine, so on that point it is not a minority opinion.

argumentFlow.label: A baraita listing Rabbi Matya's remedies
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: A baraita supports this: Rabbi Matya permits donkey meat for yerakon, the dog's liver lobe for a bite, and mouth medicine on Shabbat; the Rabbis say 'these' have no remedial value. The Gemara asks whether 'these' excludes the tzefidna medicine, which the Rabbis concede.

argumentFlow.label: What 'these' excludes
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara concludes that the Rabbis' word 'these' excludes a different remedy - Rabbi Matya's permission to let blood for serunkhi on Shabbat, which all agree is effective - as a parallel baraita from Rabbi Yishmael son of Rabbi Yosei confirms.

theological: Tzefardea: named after the Hebrew word for frog, possibly related to a venomous
narrative: Tzefardea (snake-related illness): what it is, its symptoms,

requiresUnderstanding: Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Tzefidna is a minor gum problem, so Rabbi Yochanan relied on a lone lenient opinion.
misconceptions.correction: Tzefidna spreads to the intestines and is life-threatening; moreover the Sages agreed with Rabbi Matya specifically about mouth medicine, so it is not a lone minority view.

visualizableElements.name: Tzefardea (snake-related illness): what it is, its

quizSeeds.question: Why is treating tzefidna permitted on Shabbat if it begins as a gum ailment?
quizSeeds.answer: Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak explains that tzefidna descends from the mouth to the intestines and is genuinely life-threatening, so treating it overrides Shabbat.

quizSeeds.question: What does the word 'these' in the Rabbis' objection exclude?
quizSeeds.answer: It excludes a remedy the Rabbis accept - bloodletting for serunkhi on Shabbat - distinguishing it from the remedies they consider ineffective.

sugyot.finalRuling: The Gemara discusses 'tzefardea,' a serious illness apparently related to snake or frog venom, describing its symptoms and confirming that it is life-

rashiTranslations.he: למקטליה בדבר הנזרק. למ"ד רוח רעה שורה עליו לא יקרב
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the signs that indicate a dog is rabid and therefore dangerous.

rashiTranslations.he: אצלו להרגו בידים אלא זורק בו חץ או סכין והורגו: דחייף ביה.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: R. Matya ben Harash - throat pain overrides YK due to proximity to vital organs.

rashiTranslations.he: המתחכך בו: דנכית ליה. שהכלב נושכו: נישלח למאניה. יפשיט
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: tzefardea - described as a dangerous illness related to snake or frog venom.

rashiTranslations.he: בגדיו: משכא דאפא. הוא הצבוע ובלע"ז פוטויי"ש: דאפא דיכרא.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 84a.

rashiTranslations.he: צבוע זכר: בגובתא דנחשא. על ידי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 84a.

rashiTranslations.he: קנה של נחשת: דילמא חזי לבבואיה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 84a.

rashiTranslations.he: דשד. שקפץ מן הכלב עליו ומסתכן:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 84a.

rashiTranslations.he: צפידנא. חולי השינים והחניכים
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 84a.

rashiTranslations.he: ומתחיל בפה וגומר בבני מעיים
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 84a.

rashiTranslations.he: ומסוכן הוא: מטרוניתא. נכרית היתה:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 84a.

rashiTranslations.he: עבדא ליה רפואה חמשא ומעלי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 84a.

rashiTranslations.he: שבתא. יום ה' בשבת וע"ש: והאיכא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 84a.

rashiTranslations.he: חלול השם. שהיא סבורה שעבר על
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 84a.

rashiTranslations.he: שבועתו: דמגלי לה מעיקרא.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: מההיא שעתא גלי לה לא נשבעתי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: לך כי כך אמרתי: משחא דגדפא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: דאוזא. שומן מוח עצם קטן שבראש
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: כנף העוף שקורין כניי"ל סכה אותו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: על צדעיו: קשייתא דזיתי. גרעיני
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: זיתים: דלא מלו תילתא. שלא הביאו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: שליש: אמרא חדתא. על מר חדש
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: שקורין פושוי"ר: ודביק בככי דדריה.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: הדבק בשורת השינים: ממאי הוי.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: על ידי מה בא החולי הזה: מחמימי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: חמימי דחיטי. האוכל פת חמה יותר
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: מדאי: כסא דהרסנא. דגים מטוגנים
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: עם הקמח בשמן שלהן: שיורי. שעבר
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: עליו הלילה: מאי סימניה. של אותו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: חולי: כד רמי מידי בככיה. כשנותן
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: כלום בשיניו זב הדם משורת שיניו:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: ורבי יוחנן היכי עביד הכי וכו'. לא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: גרסינן לה הכא אלא במסכת ע"ז:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: כמאן עביד ר' יוחנן. לתת סם
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: בשבת בשביל חולי השינים דקאמר
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: לה אי מצריכנא מאי: שאני אומר
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: בזו. מודים לו חכמים שהיא רפואה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: לו בכלב שוטה בחצר כבד שלו:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: ירקון. חולי שפניו מוריקות: הכי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: גרסינן וחכמים אומרים באלו אין
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: בהן משום רפואה באלו למעוטי מאי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: לאו למעוטי סם. והכי קאמרי ליה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: בשתים הראשונות אין זו רפואה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains: in the first two cases [of the dispute], this is not a complete medical treatment sufficient to override Torah law...

rashiTranslations.he: גמורה לעבור עליהן דבר תורה
rashiTranslations.en: ...so as to transgress the Torah prohibition...

rashiTranslations.he: ולמעוטי סם דיש בו משום רפואה:
rashiTranslations.en: ...and the Tanna's exclusion is to exclude a drug that does constitute genuine medical treatment.

rashiTranslations.he: לא למעוטי מקיזין דם לסרונכי.
rashiTranslations.en: Not to exclude bloodletting for srunki disease...

rashiTranslations.he: בשבת לעולם אכולי הנך פליגי ומאי
rashiTranslations.en: ...on Shabbat - all the Sages dispute regarding all those cases; and the phrase 'among these' is used because...

rashiTranslations.he: באלו משום דשמעוה לר' מתיא דאמר
rashiTranslations.en: ...they had heard R. Mattia ben Harash's ruling and then said there are further cases beyond it.

rashiTranslations.he: אכתי אחריתי: לסרונכי. חולי ששמו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi on 'for srunki': srunki is the name of a disease called...

rashiTranslations.he: אשטרנגליו"ן הוא בו"ן מלנט ובההיא
rashiTranslations.en: ...estrangalion (a urinary blockage condition); in that case [R. Mattia's leniency] he agrees.

rashiTranslations.he: מודה ליה: ה"ג הכי נמי מסתברא
rashiTranslations.en: This is the correct reading of the text. It also seems reasonable from what follows...

rashiTranslations.he: דתניא שלשה דברים אמר רבי אלעזר
rashiTranslations.en: ...for a baraita teaches: R. Elazar ben Azariah said in the name of R. Mattia ben Harash -

rashiTranslations.he: בן עזריה משום ר' מתיא בן חרש
rashiTranslations.en: one performs bloodletting for srunki on Shabbat; one bitten by a mad dog is fed from the dog's liver lobe;

rashiTranslations.he: מקיזין דם לסרונכי בשבת ומי שנשכו כלב שוטה מאכילין אותו מחצר כבד שלו והחושש בפיו מטילין לו סם בשבת וחכמים אומרים
rashiTranslations.en: one who has mouth pain is given medicine on Shabbat. But the Sages say: in these [first two] cases there is no [normal] prohibition being overridden -

rashiTranslations.he: באלו אין בהם משום רפואה באלו למעוטי מאי לאו אתרתי בתרייתא ולמעוטי דרישא לא אתרתי דרישא ולמעוטי דסיפא:
rashiTranslations.en: the question is: 'in these' to exclude what? Not the first two cases excluding the last, but the first two cases excluding the rest.

rashiTranslations.he: תא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi opens the next proof: 'come [and hear]' (ta shema), introducing a further source.
